terminologies.meaning: A framework that reformulates layers to learn residual functions F(x) = H(x) - x instead of directly learning the underlying mapping H(x), making it easier to optimize very deep networks.
Connections that skip one or more layers; in this work they perform identity mapping and add neither extra parameters nor computational complexity.
A shortcut that directly copies its input to its output (x → x), introducing no additional parameters or computation.
A residual building block composed of three layers (1×1, 3×3, 1×1 convolutions) where the 1×1 layers reduce and then restore dimensions, leaving the 3×3 layer as a low‑dimensional bottleneck.
The phenomenon where deeper plain networks exhibit higher training error and lower accuracy, not caused by overfitting but by optimization difficulty.

figures.description: Training error (left) and test error (right) on CIFAR‑10 with 20‑layer and 56‑layer 'plain' networks; deeper network has higher training error, similar phenomena on ImageNet.
Residual learning: a building block.
Example network architectures for ImageNet. Left: VGG‑19 model; Middle: a plain network with 34 parameter layers; Right: a residual network with 34 parameter layers.
Training on ImageNet. Thin curves denote training error, bold curves denote validation error of the center crops. Left: plain networks of 18 and 34 layers. Right: ResNets of 18 and 34 layers.
A deeper residual function F for ImageNet. Left: a building block (on 56×56 feature maps) as in Fig. 3 for ResNet‑34. Right: a 'bottleneck' building block for ResNet‑50/101/152.
Training on CIFAR‑10. Dashed lines denote training error, bold lines denote testing error. Left: plain networks. Middle: ResNets. Right: ResNets with 110 and 1202 layers.
Standard deviations (std) of layer responses on CIFAR‑10. Shows that ResNets have generally smaller responses than plain counterparts.
Object detection improvements on MS COCO using Faster R-CNN and ResNet‑101.

algorithms.goal: Ease the training of substantially deeper neural networks.
algorithms.process: Reformulate each set of stacked layers to learn a residual function F(x) = H(x) – x, then add the input x back (y = F(x) + x).

algorithms.goal: Reduce computational cost while enabling very deep networks.
algorithms.process: Replace a 2‑layer residual block with a 3‑layer block consisting of 1×1, 3×3, and 1×1 convolutions; the 1×1 layers reduce and then restore channel dimensions, leaving the 3×3 layer as a low‑dimensional bottleneck.

After the initial regression, pool a new feature from the regressed box, obtain a new classification score and box, combine these 300 new predictions with the original 300, apply non‑maximum suppression (IoU 0.3) and box voting.

algorithms.goal: Boost detection performance by providing image‑level context.
algorithms.process: Pool a global feature from the full‑image convolutional map using spatial‑pyramid pooling, concatenate it with each region’s feature before the final classification and box‑regression layers, and train the whole network end‑to‑end.

algorithms.goal: Increase detection accuracy by leveraging multiple image resolutions.
algorithms.process: Compute convolutional feature maps on an image pyramid (shorter sides 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 pixels), perform RoI pooling on two adjacent scales, merge the resulting features with a maxout operation, and use the combined representation for classification and regression.

weakly_supported: Figure 8 (Object detection improvements on MS COCO using Faster R-CNN and ResNet‑101) – this figure label does not appear explicitly in the provided document text.
ambiguous: Algorithm: Residual Learning – the extraction treats it as an algorithm, but in the paper it is presented more as a conceptual framework rather than a step‑by‑step algorithm.
Algorithm: Bottleneck Architecture – this is primarily an architectural design choice rather than a distinct algorithmic procedure.
missing: Batch Normalization (BN) – extensively discussed as a key component of the training pipeline.
Projection Shortcut – a shortcut option using a linear projection W_s for dimension matching.
Zero‑padding Shortcut – the shortcut variant that pads extra dimensions with zeros when dimensions increase.
Identity Shortcut – the identity‑mapping shortcut (x → x) is a core terminology not listed.
Residual Block – the building block consisting of stacked layers plus a shortcut connection.
Residual Function F (or Residual Mapping) – the function F(x) = H(x) – x that the stacked layers learn.
Plain Network (or Plain Net) – the baseline architecture without residual connections.
Residual Network (ResNet) – the name of the networks that incorporate residual learning.
Deep Residual Learning (title of the paper) – the overarching concept introduced.
Degradation problem – while mentioned, it could be highlighted as a distinct term describing the training difficulty of deeper plain nets.

terminologies.meaning: those skipping one or more layers
add neither extra parameter nor computational complexity
used to match dimensions (done by 1 × 1 convolutions)
identity mapping with extra zero entries padded for increasing dimensions
F(x) := H(x) – x
when network depth increases, accuracy gets saturated and then degrades rapidly

figures.description: Training error (left) and test error (right) on CIFAR-10 with 20-layer and 56-layer 'plain' networks. The deeper network has higher training error, and thus test error. Similar phenomena on ImageNet is presented in Fig. 4.
Residual learning: a building block.

Example network architectures for ImageNet. Left : the VGG-19 model [41] (19.6 billion FLOPs) as a reference. Middle : a plain network with 34 parameter layers (3.6 billion FLOPs). Right : a residual network with 34 parameter layers (3.6 billion FLOPs). The dotted shortcuts increase dimensions. Table 1 shows more details and other variants.

Training on ImageNet . Thin curves denote training error, and bold curves denote validation error of the center crops. Left: plain networks of 18 and 34 layers. Right: ResNets of 18 and 34 layers. In this plot, the residual networks have no extra parameter compared to their plain counterparts.
A deeper residual function F for ImageNet. Left: a building block (on 56 × 56 feature maps) as in Fig. 3 for ResNet34. Right: a 'bottleneck' building block for ResNet-50/101/152.

Training on CIFAR-10 . Dashed lines denote training error, and bold lines denote testing error. Left : plain networks. The error of plain-110 is higher than 60% and not displayed. Middle : ResNets. Right : ResNets with 110 and 1202 layers.

Standard deviations (std) of layer responses on CIFAR10. The responses are the outputs of each 3 × 3 layer, after BN and before nonlinearity. Top : the layers are shown in their original order. Bottom : the responses are ranked in descending order.

tables.title: Architectures for ImageNet. Building blocks are shown in brackets (see also Fig. 5), with the numbers of blocks stacked. Downsampling is performed by conv3 1, conv4 1, and conv5 1 with a stride of 2.

Error rates (%, 10-crop testing) on ImageNet validation. VGG-16 is based on our test. ResNet-50/101/152 are of option B that only uses projections for increasing dimensions.
Error rates (%) of single-model results on the ImageNet validation set (except † reported on the test set).
Error rates (%) of ensembles . The top-5 error is on the test set of ImageNet and reported by the test server.

Object detection mAP (%) on the PASCAL VOC 2007/2012 test sets using baseline Faster R-CNN. See also Table 10 and 11 for better results.
Object detection mAP (%) on the COCO validation set using baseline Faster R-CNN. See also Table 9 for better results.
Object detection improvements on MS COCO using Faster R-CNN and ResNet-101.
Detection results on the PASCAL VOC 2007 test set. The baseline is the Faster R-CNN system. The system 'baseline+++' include box refinement, context, and multi-scale testing in Table 9.

Detection results on the PASCAL VOC 2012 test set ( http://host.robots.ox.ac.uk:8080/leaderboard/ displaylb.php?challengeid=11&compid=4 ). The baseline is the Faster R-CNN system. The system 'baseline+++' include box refinement, context, and multi-scale testing in Table 9.
Our results (mAP, %) on the ImageNet detection dataset. Our detection system is Faster R-CNN [32] with the improvements in Table 9, using ResNet-101.

Localization error (%) on the ImageNet validation. In the column of 'LOC error on GT class' ([41]), the ground truth class is used. In the 'testing' column, '1-crop' denotes testing on a center crop of 224 × 224 pixels, 'dense' denotes dense (fully convolutional) and multi-scale testing.
Comparisons of localization error (%) on the ImageNet dataset with state-of-the-art methods.

missing: Learning rate schedule (initial LR, decay steps)
Identity mapping (as a concept separate from shortcut connections)
Projection shortcut (option B) as a distinct term
Zero‑padding shortcut (option A) as a distinct term
Algorithms: residual learning formulation (y = F(x) + x), training procedure, detection pipeline

terminologies.meaning: Normalization applied after each convolution and before activation to accelerate training.
A residual block that uses a stack of 1×1, 3×3, and 1×1 convolutions to reduce then restore dimensions.
A layer that averages spatial dimensions of feature maps to produce a single vector before the final fully‑connected layer.
Standard testing procedure that evaluates a model on ten crops of the input image.
Optimizer used for training with a mini‑batch size of 256.
Initial learning rate of 0.1, divided by 10 when error plateaus (or at specific iteration milestones such as 32k and 48k for CIFAR‑10).
L2 regularization coefficient set to 0.0001.
Momentum coefficient set to 0.9 in the SGD optimizer.
Shortcut connection that adds the input directly to the output without any parameters.
Framework that reformulates layers to learn residual functions F(x) such that the overall mapping is y = F(x) + x.
Building block defined by y = F(x, {W_i}) + x, where F is a stack of layers.
Shortcut that uses a linear projection (1×1 convolution) W_s to match dimensions when input and output sizes differ.
Shortcut that pads extra zero entries to increase dimensions without adding parameters.
Scale augmentation by random resizing of the shorter side, random 224×224 crops (or 32×32 for CIFAR‑10), horizontal flips, per‑pixel mean subtraction, and standard color augmentation.
Averaging model scores over multiple image scales (shorter side in {224, 256, 384, 480, 640}).
Object detection pipeline consisting of shared convolutional feature maps, a Region Proposal Network, and a Fast R‑CNN detection network.
Network that generates object proposals; in the localization setting it outputs per‑class scores and box regressors.
Detection network that classifies and refines proposals using RoI pooling.
Bounding‑box regression learned separately for each object class (PCR).
Iterative refinement of predicted boxes by re‑pooling features from the regressed box, recomputing scores and boxes, then applying NMS and box voting.
Incorporating a global feature obtained via Spatial Pyramid Pooling over the whole image and concatenating it with region features.
Training for up to 60 × 10⁴ iterations on ImageNet with batch size 256; on CIFAR‑10 for 64 k iterations with batch size 128; learning‑rate decay points as described.

algorithms.goal: Ease the training of very deep networks by learning residual functions instead of direct mappings.
algorithms.process: Stacked layers learn a residual mapping F(x, {W_i}); a shortcut connection adds the input x to F(x) to produce the output y.

Initialize weights as in [13]; train with mini‑batch size 256 (ImageNet) or 128 (CIFAR‑10); use learning rate 0.1, decay by factor 10 at plateau or at predefined iterations; apply weight decay 0.0001 and momentum 0.9.

algorithms.algorithm: Faster R‑CNN detection pipeline with ResNet backbone
algorithms.goal: Detect objects in images with high accuracy.
algorithms.process: Compute shared convolutional feature maps (strides ≤ 16); Region Proposal Network generates 300 proposals; RoI pooling extracts region features; Fast R‑CNN classifies and regresses boxes; optional box refinement, global context, and multi‑scale testing improve results.

algorithms.goal: Predict class‑specific bounding boxes for ImageNet localization.
algorithms.process: RPN outputs a 1000‑dimensional classification score (binary per class) and a 1000 × 4‑dimensional box regression; boxes are refined using the per‑class regressors.

algorithms.goal: Improve detection accuracy by iteratively updating box predictions.
algorithms.process: After initial regression, pool features from the regressed box, recompute classification scores and a second regression, combine original and refined predictions, apply NMS (IoU 0.3) and box voting.

algorithms.goal: Enhance detection by providing image‑level contextual information.
algorithms.process: Apply global Spatial Pyramid Pooling (single‑level) on the full‑image feature map to obtain a global feature; concatenate this with each region’s feature before the final classification and regression layers.

algorithms.goal: Boost classification and detection performance by evaluating multiple image resolutions.
algorithms.process: Resize the image to several shorter‑side lengths (e.g., {224, 256, 384, 480, 640}); compute scores for each scale; average the scores (or merge features via maxout for detection).

algorithms.goal: Increase the diversity of training data to improve generalization.
algorithms.process: Randomly sample the shorter side length within a range (e.g., [256, 480] for ImageNet); randomly crop a 224×224 (or 32×32 for CIFAR‑10) region; apply horizontal flip; subtract per‑pixel mean; use standard color augmentation.
